RICHARDS, WATSON & GERSHON
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

GLENN H. WATSON
ROBERT O. SEVERYN
HARRIS L. COHEN
CHARLES W. ARNOLD
MARK L. LAMKEN
ROBERT E. RILEY
RICHARD H. ORMEL
DAVID J. RABER
EVAN R. TAGER
MARK A. PERRY
THOMAS A. FRIEDBERG, JR.
LLOYD E. BENNETT
RANDALL E. WILLS
WILLIAM L. STRAUSS
WILLIAM O. BRAINARD
GREGORY B. DREWRY
ROBERT N. ROSENBERG
TIMOTHY L. NEUFELD
ROBERT P. DE METER
JONATHAN E. SCHREIBER
HARLEY G. FEINBERG
DONALD STEKNO
MICHAEL JENKINS
LAWRENCE J. COHEN
DAVID L. COHEN
TERESA M. TRACY
DANIEL J. HERNANDEZ
TERRY A. TRUMBULL
COLEMAN J. WALSH, JR.
SUSAN J. DRABKIN
JEFFREY A. RABIN

CURTIS L. COLEMAN
MARSHA JONES MOUTRIE
ROBERT J. NORTON
ANANDA P. SAUNDERS
SCOTT WIEBLE
JAMES R. BENNETT
THOMAS M. JIMBO
ROBERT SEAL SOWERS
RICHARD A. SEITER
SAYRE WEAVER
KEVIN G. BRENNAN
PAULETTE ROGUE
MICHAEL ESTRADA
WILLIAM J. LYON
LAURENCE E. YOUNG
DAVID A. JENSEN
CHRISTI HOGIN
STEVEN R. CIBA
DEBORAH J. GERSHON
MICHAEL S. COLONTUONO
KENNETH T. FONG
MICHAEL T. WRIGHT

RICHARD RICHARDS
(1916-1988)

THIRTY-EIGHTH FLOOR
333 SOUTH GRAND STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90071-1469
(213) 626-6484
TELECOPIER (213) 626-0078

OUR FILE NUMBER

C1380-900

May 31, 1991


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804-0807

     Re:  Request for Advice Letter on Behalf of
          City of Carson Councilwoman Vera Robles DeWitt

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     I have been asked by a member of the City Council of
the City of Carson, Vera Robles DeWitt, to obtain a formal
written advice letter regarding possible conflicts of interest
concerning her participation in decisions regarding the
regulation of mobilhome park rental rates and mobilhome park
closures in the City.

Questions

     1)  May Councilwoman DeWitt participate in decisions to
amend sections of the City's rent control ordinance that
apply generally to all mobilhome parks in the City?

     2)  May Councilwoman DeWitt participate in decisions to
amend the Municipal Code as it relates to the procedures and
requirements for mobilhome park closures and may she
participate in decisions regarding approval of specific
mobilhome park closures?

     3)  May Councilwoman DeWitt participate in decisions to
settle litigation challenging the validity of certain
provisions of the City's Mobilhome Rent Control Ordinance?

RICHARDS, WATSON & GERSHON


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
May 31, 1991
Page 2

Background

     Councilwoman DeWitt has recently purchased a mobilehome
in a mobilehome park located in the City of Carson. She paid
fair market value for the mobilehome. She is renting the space
on which the mobilehome is located on a month to month basis at
the regular rent and has not signed a written lease.

     The City of Carson currently regulates rents that
mobilehome parks may charge tenants for renting spaces in the
parks. Under the City's Ordinance, the Carson Mobilehome Rental
Review Board, which is not composed of members of the City
Council, establishes the permissible rents individually for each
park. The Board utilizes no set formula or inflationary factor
in establishing the rents, but rather a number of factors
including changes in the Consumer Price Index are considered.
Mobilehome Park Rental Review Board decisions are final and
cannot be appealed to the City Council.

     The City's Ordinance currently contains a vacancy
control provision which does not permit landlords to raise the
rent on the space upon a change of tenants. This provision has
not been amended since the adoption of California Civil Code
Section 798.17.

     As originally adopted, Section 798.17 permitted park owners to
offer homeowners a long term lease (more than 12
months) exempt from local rent control. Homeowners had the right
to accept or reject such a lease pursuant to Sections 798.17 and
798.18.

     Section 798.17 was amended, effective January 1, 1991,
to authorize park owners to require prospective purchasers of
mobilehomes to sign long term leases exempt from rent control as
a condition of tenancy. It thus removed the ability of the City
to enforce its vacancy control provision. Just recently, Senate
Bill 132 was adopted and signed by Governor Wilson as an
emergency measure effective May 10, 1991. This bill repealed
the amendment allowing park owners to require prospective
purchasers to sign long term leases exempt from rent control.

     Nonetheless, some park owners are claiming that because
prospective purchasers are not homeowners, (Civil Code §798.9)
they can be required to sign an exempt lease as a condition of
tenancy. The City's position is that this claim is erroneous
because Section 798.74 strictly limits the grounds for denying a
prospective purchaser of a mobilehome a tenancy in the park and
failure to sign an exempt lease is not a permitted ground. The
City has, with the exception of the period between January 1,

RICHARDS, WATSON & GERSHON


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
May 31, 1991
Page 3


1991 and May 10, 1991, when the law authorized park owners to
require an exempt lease, uniformly interpreted and applied its
ordinance to control rent upon a vacancy and continues to do so.

The City Council has the authority under California
Government Code Section 6563.7 and the Carson Municipal Code, to
condition mobilehome park closures by requiring park owners to
provide reasonable relocation benefits to homeowners displaced by
the closure. Pursuant to those procedures the City Council may
impose a variety of conditions upon a change of use, including
but not limited to, provision for payment of the cost of moving
mobilehomes to new sites and other reasonable costs of
relocation. The City knows of no present intention by the owner
of the mobilehome park in which Councilwoman DeWitt's home is
located to close that park, and, in fact, the owner has stated
that he has no intention of closing it in the foreseeable future.

The population of the City, as determined by the latest
census, is 83,262. There are currently 24,777 dwelling units in
the City, of which 2,632 are mobilehome spaces. Mobilehomes,
therefore, constitute approximately 11% of the number of dwelling
units in the City. The City has no figures for the number of
persons residing in mobilehomes, but it is likely that this
figure would be less than 10% of the entire City population,
since many mobilehomes are owned by retired or older persons
who do not have young children.

Discussion

In our process of analyzing the questions presented
above, we have considered several issues, which if specifically
addressed by you, will help us to advise the City and others we
represent as similar questions arise. Those issues are as
follows:

1) Is a month-to-month tenancy in the mobilehome park
space an interest in real property in view of the fact that
the landlord can terminate the tenancy only for the reasons
listed in Section 798.56 of the California Civil Code?

2) Are the conclusions in Advice Letters to Jorgensen
(No. A-90-017) and Sprague (No. A-86-260) that a mobilehome
rent control ordinance with vacancy control has the effect
of raising the fair market value of the tenant's mobilehome
still valid after adoption of the 1990 and 1991
amendments to Civil Code Section 798.17?

3) Is it foreseeable that an amendment to the
Mobilehome Park Closure Ordinance regulations in the Carson Municipal

RICHARDS, WATSON & GERSHON

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
May 31, 1991
Page 4

Code would have a material effect on Councilwoman DeWitt's
interests because the mobilehome park owner may, at some
future date, decide to close the mobilehome park, even
though there is no present intention by the City or owner,
to close the mobilehome park in which her mobilehome is
located?

4)    Does part (a)(4) of Section 18702.1 of the Cal.
Code of Regs. apply to an analysis of whether a decision to
amend the Rent Control Ordinance would have a material
effect on the public official's rent expenses, and if so,
over what period of time would the rent expenses have to
increase or decrease by at least $250?

5)    Are mobilehome park residents or owners of
mobilehomes in Carson a significant segment of the public?

Thank you for your assistance in this matter.   Please
feel free to contact me at your convenience if you need further
clarification of the questions or if you desire any additional
background information.

Very truly yours,

Kevin G. Ennis
Assistant City Attorney

KGE;pjr
1680498

sections.question:
1) May Councilwoman DeWitt participate in decisions to amend sections of the City's rent control ordinance that apply generally to all mobilehome parks in the City?

2) May Councilwoman DeWitt participate in decisions to amend the Municipal Code as it relates to the procedures and requirements for mobilehome park closures and may she participate in decisions regarding approval of specific mobilehome park closures?

3) May Councilwoman DeWitt participate in decisions to settle litigation challenging the validity of certain provisions of the City's Mobilehome Rent Control Ordinance?

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
May 31, 1991
sections.facts:
Councilwoman DeWitt has recently purchased a mobilehome in a mobilehome park located in the City of Carson. She paid fair market value for the mobilehome. She is renting the space on which the mobilehome is located on a month to month basis at the regular rent and has not signed a written lease.

The City of Carson currently regulates rents that mobilehome parks may charge tenants for renting spaces in the parks. Under the City's Ordinance, the Carson Mobilehome Rental Review Board, which is not composed of members of the City Council, establishes the permissible rents individually for each park. The Board utilizes no set formula or inflationary factor in establishing the rents, but rather a number of factors including changes in the Consumer Price Index are considered. Mobilehome Park Rental Review Board decisions are final and cannot be appealed to the City Council.

The City's Ordinance currently contains a vacancy control provision which does not permit landlords to raise the rent on the space upon a change of tenants. This provision has not been amended since the adoption of California Civil Code Section 798.17.

As originally adopted, Section 798.17 permitted park owners to offer homeowners a long term lease (more than 12 months) exempt from local rent control. Homeowners had the right to accept or reject such a lease pursuant to Sections 798.17 and 798.18.

Section 798.17 was amended, effective January 1, 1991, to authorize park owners to require prospective purchasers of mobilehomes to sign long term leases exempt from rent control as a condition of tenancy. It thus removed the ability of the City to enforce its vacancy control provision. Just recently, Senate Bill 132 was adopted and signed by Governor Wilson as an emergency measure effective May 10, 1991. This bill repealed the amendment allowing park owner to demand new mobilehome purchasers to sign long term leases exempt from rent control.

Nonetheless, some park owners are claiming that because prospective purchasers are not homeowners, (Civil Code §798.9) they can be required to sign an exempt lease as a condition of tenancy. The City's position is that this claim is erroneous because Section 798.74 strictly limits the grounds for denying a prospective purchaser of a mobilehome a tenancy in the park and failure to sign an exempt lease is not a permitted ground. The City has, with the exception of the period between January 1,

1991 and May 10, 1991, when the law authorized park owners to require an exempt lease, uniformly interpreted and applied its ordinance to control rent upon a vacancy and continues to do so.

The City Council has the authority under California Government Code Section 65863.7 and the Carson Municipal Code, to condition mobilehome park closures by requiring park owners to provide reasonable relocation benefits to homeowners displaced by the closure. Pursuant to those procedures the City Council may impose a variety of conditions upon a change of use, including but not limited to, provision for payment of the cost of moving mobilehomes to new sites and other reasonable costs of relocation. The City knows of no present intention by the owner of the mobilehome park in which Councilwoman DeWitt's home is located to close that park, and, in fact, the owner has stated that he has no intention of closing it in the foreseeable future.

The population of the City, as determined by the latest census, is 83,262. There are currently 24,777 dwelling units in the City, of which 2,632 are mobilehome spaces. Mobilehomes, therefore, constitute approximately 11% of the number of dwelling units in the City. The City has no figures for the number of persons residing in mobilehomes, but it is likely that this figure would be less than 10% of the entire City population, since many mobilehomes are owned by retired or older persons who do not have young children.

Code would have a material effect on Councilwoman DeWitt's interests because the mobilehome park owner may, at some future date, decide to close the mobilehome park, even though there is no present intention by the City or owner, to close the mobilehome park in which her mobilehome is located?
